blurb: Indian flatbread classic - soft, pillowy naan baked in a hot tandoor or oven.

prov.famous_for: The essential Indian bread - soft, slightly charred, perfect for curries
prov.consists_of: Yogurt-leavened dough baked at high heat until puffed and lightly charred
prov.name_origin: Persian nān (bread); Mughal-era introduction to India
prov.why_loved: Soft pillowy texture, slight char flavour, perfect for scooping curries
prov.method_detail: Oven - dough proved, divided, baked at 260C until puffed and charred

brief.lead: A classic Indian flatbread made with yogurt-leavened dough, baked at high heat to achieve the signature puffy, lightly charred exterior and soft, pillowy interior. Perfect for scooping up curries and gravies.

tl: Mix yeast, sugar and warm milk, let stand 10 min until foamy
Combine flour, salt, yeast mixture and yoghurt, knead 8 min until smooth and elastic
Cover bowl, leave in warm place 1 hour until doubled
Punch down, divide into 8 equal pieces, roll into balls
Roll each ball into oval teardrop shape, 5mm thick
Heat oven to 260°C with baking sheet inside
Cook 2-3 pieces at a time on hot sheet, 4-5 min until puffed and charred spots appear
Brush hot naan with garlic butter, serve immediately
steps: Activate the yeast
Mix the yeast, sugar and warm milk in a large bowl. Let stand for 10 minutes until foamy and active.
Yeast needs warmth and sugar to activate and bubble.
Mix the dough
Add the flour, salt and yoghurt to the yeast mixture. Mix to combine, then knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Kneading develops gluten for stretch and soft texture.
First prove
Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size.
Proving allows yeast to ferment and dough to rise.
Divide and shape
Punch down the dough and divide into 8 equal pieces. Roll each piece into a ball, then roll into oval teardrop shapes about 5mm thick.
Individual portions ensure even cooking and puffing.
Preheat oven
Heat oven to 260°C with a baking sheet inside. The sheet must be very hot.
Maximum heat mimics tandoor temperature for proper puff.
Bake the naan
Place 2-3 naan on the hot baking sheet. Bake for 4-5 minutes until puffed up with golden charred spots.
Hot surface creates steam pockets for pillowy texture.
Brush with butter
Mix melted butter with minced garlic. Brush hot naan generously with garlic butter and serve immediately.
Garlic butter adds authentic flavour and gloss.

blurb: Sweet corn bread — moist, golden and flecked with corn kernels, a beloved braai side and tea-time treat.

prov.popular_in: South African bakeries and home kitchens
prov.famous_for: The moist, pudding-like texture and sweet corn flavour that makes it irresistible
prov.consists_of: Fresh corn kernels folded into a quick-bread batter of flour, sugar, eggs and milk, baked until golden
prov.name_origin: 'Mealie' is Afrikaans for maize/corn; a traditional quick bread using fresh sweetcorn
prov.why_loved: Sweet, moist and golden — the perfect accompaniment to braais or enjoyed with tea
prov.method_detail: Oven — corn kernels mixed into a batter of flour, baking powder, sugar, eggs and milk, poured into a tin, baked at 180°C 35–45 min until golden and cooked

brief.lead: A beloved South African quick bread, moist and golden with fresh corn kernels throughout. Sweet, pudding-like texture makes it perfect alongside braais or enjoyed with butter and tea.

tl: Heat oven to 180°C and grease a 23x9cm loaf tin
Cut kernels from cobs, about 250 g
Sift flour, baking powder and salt into large bowl
Whisk eggs and sugar until pale, add milk and melted butter
Fold wet into dry, then fold in corn kernels until just combined
Pour into tin, bake 35-45 min until golden and skewer comes out clean
Cool in tin 10 min, then turn out
steps: Preheat oven
Heat oven to 180°C and generously grease a 23x9cm loaf tin with butter.
Proper preparation prevents sticking and ensures even baking.
Prepare corn
Cut the kernels from the corn cobs. You should have about 250 g of fresh corn kernels.
Fresh corn provides moisture and natural sweetness.
Mix dry ingredients
Sift the cake flour, baking powder and salt into a large mixing bowl.
Sifting aerates flour and distributes leavening evenly.
Combine wet ingredients
Whisk the eggs and sugar until pale and thick. Add the milk and melted butter, whisking to combine.
Whisking adds air for lighter texture.
Fold together
Fold the wet mixture into the dry ingredients until just combined. Gently fold in the corn kernels.
Minimal mixing keeps bread tender; corn stays intact.
Bake the bread
Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Bake for 35-45 minutes until golden and a skewer comes out clean.
Even baking sets the structure and cooks through.
Cool and serve
Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack. Serve warm with butter.
Brief cooling sets the crumb for slicing.

blurb: Braai bread at its best — yeasted dough balls cooked over hot coals, crisp outside and pillowy soft within.

prov.famous_for: The smoky flavour and crisp-grilled exterior only open-coals can provide
prov.consists_of: Simple yeasted dough rolled into balls, grilled over hot coals until puffy and golden brown
prov.name_origin: Afrikaans for 'griddle cake', adapted for braai coals as campfire bread
prov.why_loved: Fresh bread straight from the fire, torn with butter beside the braai — simple perfection
prov.method_detail: Fire — dough proved 1 hour, formed into balls, grilled over moderate coals 8–12 min per side until golden and hollow-sounding, served warm

brief.lead: The ultimate braai bread — simple yeasted dough balls cooked directly over hot coals for a smoky, charred exterior and pillowy soft interior. Torn fresh from the fire with melting butter, it's braai perfection.

tl: Mix yeast, sugar and warm water, let stand 10 min until foamy
Combine flour, salt, yeast mixture, milk and butter, knead 8 min until smooth
Cover bowl, leave in warm place 1 hour until doubled
Build moderate bed of coals, let flames die down
Divide dough into 8 pieces, roll into tight balls
Place on grid over moderate coals, grill 8-12 min per side until golden and hollow-sounding
Brush with melted butter immediately, serve warm
steps: Activate the yeast
Mix the yeast, sugar and warm water in a large bowl. Let stand for 10 minutes until foamy.
Yeast needs warmth and sugar to activate and bubble.
Mix the dough
Add the flour, salt, warm milk and melted butter to the yeast mixture. Mix and knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Kneading develops gluten for structure and chew.
First prove
Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size.
Proving allows yeast to ferment and dough to rise.
Prepare the coals
Build a moderate bed of coals and let the flames die down. The coals should be glowing with no active flames.
Moderate, even heat prevents burning and ensures proper cooking.
Shape the balls
Punch down the dough and divide into 8 equal pieces. Roll each piece into a tight ball.
Tight balls create interior structure for even grilling.
Grill the roosterkoek
Place the dough balls on a braai grid over moderate coals. Grill for 8-12 minutes per side until golden brown and hollow-sounding when tapped.
Direct fire contact creates smoky char and pillowy puff.
Brush and serve
Brush the hot roosterkoek generously with melted butter (add garlic or herbs if desired). Serve immediately alongside the braai.
Butter adds richness and gloss; garlic creates authentic flavour.
